title: AI-assisted coding interview format: 20min live session with coding agent
description:
## Concept

An interview format where the candidate gets 20 minutes with a coding agent (e.g., Claude Code) while the interviewer observes.

## What It Evaluates

| Skill | How observed |
| ----- | ------------ |
| Agent fluency | How naturally they interact with the AI — prompting, correcting, steering |
| Tool efficiency | Keyboard shortcuts, command usage, workflow speed |
| Judgment | When they override the agent vs. trust it; what they verify |
| Innovation | Creative use of agent capabilities; novel approaches to the problem |
| Communication | How they explain their thinking while working |
| Code literacy | Can they read and evaluate AI-generated code quickly? |

## Why This Matters

Traditional coding interviews test memorized algorithms. This format tests **how effectively someone works with AI tools** — which is the actual job now.

## Open Questions

- What task to give? Open-ended feature? Bug fix? Refactor?
- Which agent/tool to standardize on, or let candidate choose?
- Scoring rubric — how to compare candidates fairly?
- Does this disadvantage candidates unfamiliar with a specific agent?
- Should the candidate explain as they go, or review at the end?
notes:
## Additional Evaluation Criteria: Work Habits

Beyond coding skill, this format reveals meta-skills invisible in traditional interviews:

| Habit | What to observe |
| ----- | --------------- |
| Scope awareness | When they discover the task is part of a bigger issue, do they note it and stay focused, or rabbit-hole? |
| Scalable planning | Do they break work into steps before coding, or dive in? |
| Reaction to surprise | How they handle unexpected findings (bug leads to design flaw, etc.) |
| Communication instinct | Do they explain what they found, or silently context-switch? |
| Tool discipline | Do they commit incrementally, leave notes, track loose ends? |

This may be more valuable than the coding itself — a normal coding session tests "can you solve X" while this tests "how do you work."

title: Agent apps — unified plugin/extension system with dependencies for agent harnesses
description: Superseded by GitHub #67 (agent extensions — unified extension system with dependencies for LLM agent harnesses). See: https://github.com/PabloLION/free-idea/issues/67

title: AI-powered chip die shot analyzer: image to abstract block diagram
description:
## Concept

Software that takes a chip die shot (photograph/microscope image of a CPU/GPU die) and produces an abstracted block diagram showing functional regions.

### Input

- Die shot image (microscope photo of a silicon chip)
- Could also be circuit board images

### Output

- Abstracted map/diagram identifying functional blocks (CPU cores, GPU cores, cache, memory controller, I/O, etc.)
- Interactive: user can point to a region and ask "what is this?" and get an educated guess

### Inspiration

- Human experts (not necessarily top-tier) can visually identify functional regions on die shots by recognizing patterns (regular arrays = cache/memory, repeated blocks = cores, etc.)
- Goal: automate this recognition process
- Reference: https://youtu.be/kXX9n62N72s?t=378

### Key Features

- Image → abstract block diagram conversion
- Interactive Q&A about specific regions
- Pattern recognition for common structures (SRAM arrays, logic blocks, I/O pads, etc.)

## Open Questions

- Training data: are labeled die shots available publicly?
- Scope: just identification, or also reverse engineering functionality?
- Target users: hardware enthusiasts, journalists, competitive analysis teams?
- How detailed should the output be? (top-level blocks vs. sub-block detail)

## Status

Idea phase — needs further review and detail from creator.

title: LLM plays games — auto-play Wordle, puzzles, word games
description: LLM agents that actually play games like Wordle, puzzles, word games, etc. Different from fi-46 (daily login automation) — this is about the agent understanding and playing the game itself.

title: Remove 'feat:' prefix from GitHub issue titles
description: Many issues in free-idea repo carry 'feat:' prefix from conventional commits style, which is not appropriate for idea-tracking issues. Batch cleanup across affected issues.

title: Skills with CLI will replace MCP
description: Idea originated in 2025 when skills rolled out for Claude Code. Formalized when skills became an open standard. Core thesis: skills + CLI can do everything MCP servers do, but with better ergonomics — no separate server process, no transport layer, just callable tools. MCP adds unnecessary complexity for most use cases. Skills are more composable, easier to distribute, and already integrated into the agent harness.

title: Pen motion reading AI — infer text from pen kinematics without seeing marks
description:
## Core Idea

Observer sees pen kinematics only (camera from a distance, pen-mounted IMU, hand-mounted IMU, EMG, etc.) — cannot see paper or ink marks. Task: reconstruct the text that was written.

Analogous to lip reading: infer the information channel from its motor shadow, without access to the primary output.

## Distinct From

- **Standard handwriting OCR** (#17) — sees the marks on paper
- **Air writing / gesture input** — no paper, motion in empty space
- **Smart pens (Livescribe, etc.)** — use infrared dot pattern on special paper, essentially digital ink capture
- **Acoustic keystroke side-channel** — adjacent genre (inferring input from unintended signals), different modality

## Sensor Options

- External camera (from above, side, or across a room through a window)
- Pen-mounted IMU / accelerometer / gyroscope
- Hand or wrist IMU (smartwatch-style)
- EMG / forearm muscle signals
- Acoustic signature of pen-on-paper friction

## Open Questions

- Which sensor modality gives the best character-level accuracy?
- Can a consumer camera at 2-5 meters reconstruct legible text from hand motion?
- What's the security/surveillance threat model? Can this be done covertly?
- Training data: need paired (motion, text) examples — who has these?

## Status

**Partial draft.** Needs: prior-art research (check gesture recognition, EMG handwriting, surveillance side-channel literature), feasibility notes, existing projects inventory. Flesh out before publishing to GitHub.

title: Uber Eats will become an insurance company
description: Thesis: Uber Eats has the data, logistics network, and risk modeling capability to pivot into insurance. Details TBD.

title: Power Toys for Mac — expose hidden macOS settings with GUI
description: macOS System Settings hides many options that are accessible via command line (defaults write, etc.). A Power Toys equivalent for Mac that surfaces these hidden settings in a GUI. Similar to Windows PowerToys but for macOS. Example: showing battery percentage in menu bar extras requires navigating to a non-obvious location in System Settings.

title: Run #55 research sections and reply to m13v
description: Issue #55 has 5 collapsible 'Needs Internet Research' sections not yet done. Also owes m13v a reply with the shrink skill link once published. Deferred until shrink skill is ready to share publicly.

title: Agent harness permission model is confusing — needs redesign
description: Claude Code's permission model across scopes (managed, CLI, local project, shared project, user) plus skill allowed_tools and subagent tools field is poorly documented and confusing. Key issues: (1) unclear if subagent tools can widen or only restrict, (2) skill allowed_tools is additive but cannot override denies — this interaction is not obvious, (3) deny-wins-globally policy means lower scopes can never grant what higher scopes deny, but the documentation doesn't make this clear. Related to fi-4g6 (agent extensions).

title: Hackathon voting weighted by money paid — judge/payer profiles
description: Hackathon judging system where vote weight scales with financial contribution. Judges are payers — whoever funds more has more weight. Need profiles for these judge/payers. Details TBD.
